text: The patient is positioned in a left lateral decubitus position. The Da Vinci X robotic system is docked from the patient's head. Four robotic ports are placed along the virtual subcostal lobotomy line, and two laparoscopic ports are positioned in the left flank and across the umbilicus. The second surgeon stands between the patient's legs.

The right lobe of the liver is mobilized, and the supra-hepatic vein is sectioned using a robotic hook and bipolar forceps. The posterior glissandian pedicle is visible. A cholecystectomy is performed, with the cystic duct and artery being sectioned between clips. The right coronary ligament is sectioned, providing access to the tripartite inferior vena cava.

The surgeon dissects accessory supra-hepatic veins, sectioning larger veins between hemlock clips and coagulating smaller veins with bipolar forceps. The robotic arm aids in exposing the entire face of the inferior vena cava, facilitating dissection and control of the veins using the robotic bipolar and hook.
The surgeon dissects a branch for segment VII, securing it between two hemlock clips. The mobilization of the right lobe is completed by sectioning the superior part of the right triangular ligament.

Intraoperative ultrasonography is used to visualize the glissane branches of segment VI and VII. The surgeon begins with a caudate lobe-first approach, sectioning the caudate lobe along the axis of the inferior vena cava. The robotic console surgeon employs a robotic hook and bipolar forceps, while the second surgeon uses a vacuum irrigator to clean the field. At the end of the caudate lobe transection, the glissane branches of segment VI and VII are visible, and a surgical loop is placed on the segment VII glissane branch.
The surgeon performs a clamping test to ensure the demarcation line and checks for portal flow in the anterior section. The segment VII glissian branch is sectioned between two hemlock clips.

The surgeon continues the anatomical resection of segment seven, using irrigation to enhance the efficiency of the bipolar forceps and prevent tissue adhesion. The parenchymal transection proceeds along the axis of the right portal sulcus, revealing the right hepatic vein, which is dissected towards its junction with the inferior vena cava. An ultrasonic dissector is utilized for its atraumatic properties during this part of the procedure.

The surgical team uses arm four and three hands of the second surgeon to perform a parenchymal transection with four hands. Hemostasis and biliostasis are achieved using bipolar forceps and a hook. Branches of segment VII leading to the right suprahepatic vein are dissected and sectioned between hematoclips for hemostasis. Robotic device articulation aids in maintaining alignment with the right hepatic veins. A surgical loop is positioned behind the transection line for enhanced exposure.

The surgeon completes the dissection, repairing bleeding from the right hepatic vein with a suture. The stable vision and tremor filtration aid in controlling the bleeding. The suprahepatic vein, segment VII glissane branch, and inferior vena cava are visible at the end of the resection.
